one_line_takeaway: Text LLM watermarks (UMD, Unigram) are far more fragile on code than on prose: cheap semantics-preserving AST edits like dead-code insertion and try/catch wrapping drop detection TPR from ~0.8 to as low as 0.01-0.34, showing code needs its own robust watermarking.
key_finding: Simple semantics-preserving edits drastically reduce watermark detectability without altering functionality. On LlamA-7B, UMD TPR falls from 0.79 (Original) to 0.22 under WrapTryCatch and 0.34 under Mixed; the Unigram scheme (marketed as edit-robust) is even more fragile, dropping from 0.76 to 0.01 under AddDeadCode, 0.11 under WrapTryCatch, and 0.14 under Mixed. Even the simplest edits (Rename, InsertPrint) cut TPR by at least ~1.3x on Llama-7B and ~1.4x on CodeLlama-7B. CodeLlama-7B shows the same pattern (UMD 0.82 -> 0.28 WrapTryCatch; Unigram 0.72 -> 0.03 AddDeadCode). Detectability degrades monotonically with the number of transformations d (Figure 1): 5 WrapTryCatch edits drive UMD to TPR 0.22 and Unigram to 0.11, and AddDeadCode/WrapTryCatch need fewer edits than Rename/InsertPrint to halve the TPR. Table 1 shows AddDeadCode changes ~36% of tokens and WrapTryCatch ~27%, well above the ~50% text-watermark-removal threshold once a few are combined. Conclusion: current text-oriented LLM watermarks do not transfer robustly to code, motivating code-specific (e.g., syntax-tree-level) watermarking.
core_contribution: The paper presents what the authors claim is the first in-depth study of the robustness of existing LLM watermarking schemes when applied to LLM-generated CODE (Python specifically). Its own contribution is an attack: an AST-walking algorithm (Algorithm 1, PERTURB) that applies a sequence of d randomized, semantics-preserving program transformations to watermarked code, and an empirical demonstration that these edits collapse watermark detectability. The key thesis is that watermarks shown to be robust in the plain-text setting are far more fragile for code, because code admits cheap semantics-preserving edits (variable renaming, dead-code insertion, print insertion, try/catch wrapping) that touch large fractions of tokens without changing program behavior. Note: the two watermark schemes evaluated (UMD, Unigram) and the SWEET code-watermark are prior work / cited baselines, NOT contributions of this paper; the paper's own artifact is the transformation attack and the evaluation.
threat_model: Adversary (a user who obtained watermarked code from an LLM) wants to evade detection so that AI-generated code is not flagged (e.g., to hide plagiarism or malicious-code provenance). Capability assumption stated explicitly in Section 2.2: the adversary has only black-box input-output access to the model M and has NO knowledge of the watermarking algorithm, the cryptographic/hash key zeta, or the detection threshold. The adversary applies a series of d semantics-preserving transformations to the watermarked program so that Detect(y_A, zeta) returns 0 while preserving functionality. Prior work is cited as claiming at least 50% of tokens must be modified to remove a text watermark; the paper argues code makes reaching that threshold trivial.
limitations: Scope is narrow: only Python code, only two watermark schemes (UMD and Unigram), only 7B-parameter models (LlamA-7B, CodeLlama-7B), and a single benchmark (HumanEval). Notably the paper does NOT evaluate SWEET (Lee et al. 2023), the entropy-thresholding scheme specifically designed for code that it discusses in Related Work, so the 'code watermarks are fragile' claim is not tested against the state-of-the-art code-specific defense. The baseline detectability itself is weak: individual ~100-token completions give TPR<0.3, and a usable baseline is only achieved by artificially aggregating detection over 3 functions and using a strict z>3 threshold, which is an evaluation choice that inflates the starting TPR. Transformations are randomized and results averaged over only 3 runs; no variance/confidence intervals are reported. The paper is short (a workshop-scale study) and proposes no defense of its own beyond urging future work toward AST-based watermarking. Functional equivalence is asserted by construction of the transformations rather than validated by re-running HumanEval unit tests, so 'semantics-preserving' rests on the transformation design.

quantitative_results: Table 1 mean proportion of tokens changed: AddDeadCode 0.36, InsertPrint 0.12, Rename 0.05, WrapTryCatch 0.27, Mixed 0.22
Llama-7B UMD TPR: Original 0.79 (FPR 0), Rename 0.57, AddDeadCode 0.38, InsertPrint 0.58, WrapTryCatch 0.22, Mixed 0.34
Llama-7B Unigram TPR: Original 0.76, Rename 0.20, AddDeadCode 0.01, InsertPrint 0.32, WrapTryCatch 0.11, Mixed 0.14
CodeLlama-7B UMD TPR: Original 0.82, Rename 0.70, AddDeadCode 0.45, InsertPrint 0.61, WrapTryCatch 0.28, Mixed 0.35
CodeLlama-7B Unigram TPR: Original 0.72, Rename 0.25, AddDeadCode 0.03, InsertPrint 0.26, WrapTryCatch 0.09, Mixed 0.12
Figure 1 at d=5 WrapTryCatch: UMD TPR 0.22, Unigram TPR 0.11
Baseline (aggregating 3 function completions, reject null if z>3): TPR>0.70, FPR<=0.01; individual ~100-token completions yield TPR<0.3
Simple transforms (Rename, InsertPrint) reduce TPR by at least 1.3x on Llama-7B and 1.4x on CodeLlama-7B
Watermark params: green-list fraction gamma=0.25; Unigram strength delta=2; d=5 transformations applied sequentially; results averaged over 3 randomized runs
baselines_compared: UMD watermark (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023a) - red/green list biased sampling, z-test detection
Unigram watermark (Zhao et al. 2023) - fixed red/green split, claimed edit-robust, z-statistic detection
